Summary: This study investigates the utility of static and dynamic 18F-DOPA PET radiomics for differentiating high-grade glioma (HGG) progression from treatment-related changes (TRC). The study compares the diagnostic performance of radiomic models to the current PET imaging standard of care, finding that radiomics provide only marginal additional value over classical analysis based on TBR values. The analysis included data from two institutions and employed various machine learning models.

Condition1.reason: The study involves segmentation of tumor VOIs for feature extraction, as stated in the methods section: "Healthy brain and tumor VOI segmentations were performed by a nuclear physician (L.R.) using LifeX software".

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed by a nuclear physician, indicating that it was not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features from static TBR and dynamic TTP parametric images, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features and clinical data organized for model training and evaluation.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning. It relies on hand-crafted feature extraction and machine learning models.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines eligibility criteria for patient selection, including histological confirmation of HGG and a minimum interval between radiation therapy and PET acquisition.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on histopathology or clinical-radiology follow-up based on RANO criteria, which is a well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from two different institutions (CHRU of Nancy and Pitié-Salpêtrière hospital in Paris, France).

Item5.reason: The imaging data source (18F-FDOPA PET) is clinically translatable as it is recommended by RANO for glioma assessment.

Item6.reason: The study reports the imaging protocol with acquisition parameters for each of the four different imaging systems used.

Item7.reason: The study includes a minimum 3-month interval between the end of radiation therapy and the 18F-FDOPA PET acquisition to reduce false positives, and the final diagnoses were determined from histopathology or clinical-radiology follow-up during the 6-month follow-up period.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of LifeX software and a semi-automatic segmentation based on a threshold of 1.6 of mean standardized uptake value of healthy brain (SUVmean) to determine tumor VOIs.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed by a nuclear physician (L.R.), not by a single reader or automated tool in the test set.

Item11.reason: The study describes the image pre-processing techniques, including resampling to a consistent voxel size, correction for patient movements, and denoising using the HYPR-LR method.

Item12.reason: The study used the pyradiomics package for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (statistical, histogram-based, local-intensity, and textural features) and mentions the use of absolute discretization with fixed bin sizes. It also refers to the IBSI guidelines for radiomic feature extraction.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of non-robust features through test-retest, reproducibility analysis, or stability analysis.

Item15.reason: The study performs dimensionality reduction using hierarchical clustering based on an absolute spearman correlation coefficient (SCC).

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size using statistical means or empirical evidence.

Item17.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study splits the data into training and test sets from different hospital centers (Nancy and Paris, respectively). All transformations and algorithms were fitted using only the training set and were subsequently applied to the test set.

Item19.reason: The study corrects for data harmonization with the modified ComBat method to remove effects introduced by the use of different PET systems.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, precision, F1 score, and balanced accuracy.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures such as 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the performance metrics.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform a uni-parametric imaging evaluation or provide a justification for the use of multi-parametric imaging by comparing its performance to uni-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study compares the radiomic models to a reference model based on TBRmean, which is representative of the clinical practice.

Item25.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the models with a simple baseline reference model such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training and/or validation sets.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution (external testing).

Item28.reason: The study mentions that all the extracted data are provided in a Supplementary Table S1.

Item29.reason: The study does not explicitly state that all scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling are shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention that the final model is shared in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
